coredata.dc:title: Mesoporous silica imprinted carbon dots for the selective fluorescent detection of triclosan
coredata.dc:creator: Bhogal, Shikha
Mohiuddin, Irshad
Malik, Ashok Kumar
Brown, Richard J.C.
Heynderickx, Philippe M.
Kim, Ki-Hyun
Kaur, Kuldeep
coredata.dc:description: A molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor built as a mesoporous structured silica imprinted layer on the surface of carbon dots (CDs@m-MIP) was employed for the selective detection of triclosan (TRI). The fluorescence of this CDs@m-MIP was affected sensitively and selectively by TRI via an electron transfer-induced fluorescence quenching mechanism with a detection limit of TRI at 1.08 nM (range 1.72–138 nM) under the optimum setup (e.g., pH, response time, and CDs@m-MIP dose). This approach was used successfully to detect TRI in real water samples (e.g., sewage, river, and tap water). The recoveries of TRI were satisfactory in spiked river and tap water (in 94.7–99.5 %). The outcome of this research is thus expected to help develop highly efficient fluorescent sensing systems towards diverse hazardous compounds including TRI.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Mesoporous silica
Imprinted polymer
Triclosan
Fluorescent sensor
electron transfer induced mechanism

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst misctext otherkwds primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of mesoporous silica imprinted and non-imprinted carbon dots
Synthesis of meso-porous silica imprinted carbon dots (CDs@m-MIP) and non-imprinted carbon dots (CDs@m-NIP)
Nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherm analysis
Sensitivity of the CDs@m-MIP and CDs@m-NIP sensors towards TRI
Selectivity of the CDs@m-MIP and CDs@m-NIP
Stability and analytical performance of the CDs@m-MIP sensor
Reproducibility and regeneration of CDs@m-MIP sensor
Practical application of CDs@m-MIP for TRI detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Chemistry, Aligarh Muslim University
Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors (SB, IM, and AKM) acknowledge the UGC-SAP and Chemistry Department, Punjabi University, Patiala, for providing lab and instrument facilities. AKM thanks the UGC, New Delhi for a mid-career award grant. KK thanks Mata Gujri College, Fatehgarh Sahib, Punjab for providing lab facilities. KHK acknowledges support provided by a grant from the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science and ITC (MSIT) of the Korean government (Grant No: 2021R1A3B1068304). PMH would like to thank the Research and Development Program of Ghent University Global Campus (GUGC), Korea.

The authors (SB, IM, and AKM) acknowledge the UGC-SAP and Chemistry Department, Punjabi University, Patiala, for providing lab and instrument facilities. AKM thanks the UGC, New Delhi for a mid-career award grant. KK thanks Mata Gujri College, Fatehgarh Sahib, Punjab for providing lab facilities. KHK acknowledges support provided by a grant from the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science and ITC (MSIT) of the Korean government (Grant No: 2021R1A3B1068304 ). PMH would like to thank the Research and Development Program of Ghent University Global Campus (GUGC), Korea.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of CD-based materials prepared for sensing TRI: (a) CDs, (b) CDs@m-MIP with TRI, and (c) CDs@m-MIP without TRI.

HRTEM images of CD-based fluorescent material synthesized for the sensing of TRI: (a and b) CDs with spherical morphology and an average diameter of 2–5 nm, (c and d) CDs@m-MIP, (e and f) CDs@m-NIP with spherical morphology and average diameters of 60–80 nm.
Thermogravimetric analysis of synthesized fluorescent material when heating from 35 °C to 800 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere: (a) CDs and (b) CDs@m-MIP.

Fluorescence emission spectra of CD-based fluorescent materials: (a) CDs@m-MIP at different excitation wavelengths (330–400 nm). (b) CDs@m-MIP with TRI, CDs@m-MIP without TRI, and CDs@m-NIP. Excitation wavelength: 350 nm; emission wavelength: 450 nm; slit widths of excitation and emission were both 10 nm.
A facile surface imprinting synthesis strategy of CDs@m-MIP based on an electron transfer induced fluorescence quenching mechanism for fluorescence detection of triclosan.

Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs based fluorescent materials by adding different concentrations of TRI in the range of 0–138 nM: (a) CDs@m-MIP and (b) CDs@m-NIP. Excitation wavelength: 350 nm; emission wavelength: 450 nm; slit widths of excitation and emission were both 10 nm.

Stern Volmer plot for CD-based fluorescent materials with different concentrations of TRI in the range of 1.72–138 nM: (a) CDs@m-MIP and (b) CDs@m-NIP. Excitation wavelength: 350 nm; emission wavelength: 450 nm; and 10 nm for both slit widths of excitation and emission. Error bars indicate the standard deviation of three repeated measurements.
Determination of triclosan in different water samples (sewage water, tap, and river water) by the proposed CDs@m-MIP sensor.

row.entry: B) Real samples spiked with TRI

head.ce:title: Mesoporous silica imprinted carbon dots for the selective fluorescent detection of triclosan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor built as a mesoporous structured silica imprinted layer on the surface of carbon dots (CDs@m-MIP) was employed for the selective detection of triclosan (TRI). The fluorescence of this CDs@m-MIP was affected sensitively and selectively by TRI via an electron transfer-induced fluorescence quenching mechanism with a detection limit of TRI at 1.08 nM (range 1.72–138 nM) under the optimum setup (e.g., pH, response time, and CDs@m-MIP dose). This approach was used successfully to detect TRI in real water samples (e.g., sewage, river, and tap water). The recoveries of TRI were satisfactory in spiked river and tap water (in 94.7–99.5 %). The outcome of this research is thus expected to help develop highly efficient fluorescent sensing systems towards diverse hazardous compounds including TRI.

ce:section.ce:para: Triclosan (TRI) [5-chloro-2-(2,4-dichlorophenoxy) phenol] is a phenyl ether with a broad spectrum of antimicrobial and antifungal activities (Montaseri and Forbes, 2016). It is widely used in a variety of household and personal care products such as detergents, toothpastes, shampoos, lotions, and soaps (Motia et al., 2019). The European Community Cosmetic Directive and the US Food and Drug Agency (USFDA) regulate the content of TRI in personal care products to below 0.3 % of product mass (Rodricks et al., 2010; Directive, 2007). TRI has been detected frequently in a variety of environments, including surface water, river sediment, and bio-solids (Wang and Liang, 2021; Xin et al., 2019). At higher concentrations (e.g., ≥4.8 nM), TRI may exert toxic biochemical effects on species while harming the environment by interfering with the biochemical cycle, ecosystem structure, and functioning of organisms (Wang and Liang, 2021; Dhillon et al., 2015). Many health problems are reported to be associated with TRI including endocrine disruption, antibiotic resistance, the development of carcinogenic products, and allergies (Xin et al., 2019). Because of such concerns, many analytical procedures have been developed to detect TRI such as the use of chromatography-mass spectrometry (Alshishani et al., 2019; Arismendi et al., 2019), capillary electrophoresis (Zhang et al., 2014), spectrofluorimetry (Du et al., 2019), and electrochemical methods (Saljooqi et al., 2020). Fluorescence-based approaches have garnered a lot of interest due to their many beneficial characteristics such as high sensitivity, simplicity of fabrication, fast response, low use of organic solvents, and low cost.

Carbon dots (CDs) are well-known for their multiple beneficial properties such as exceptional photoluminescence properties, ease of preparation, high water solubility, minimal cytotoxicity, high thermal stability, and remarkable photo-stability. CDs are superior fluorophores when compared to semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes, which also suffer from toxicity and photo-bleaching concerns (Kong et al., 2015). The most common methods used for the synthesis of CDs include hydrothermal and microwave assisted methods that involve the carbonization of different organic carbon precursors such as glucose, sucrose, glycol, glycerol, and citric acid (Bhogal et al., 2020).

The optical properties of CDs are ideally suited for sensing a variety of chemical entities ranging from simple organic molecules to complicated structural frameworks containing charged and neutral analytes (Ansi et al., 2020). However, as the applicability of CD-based sensors is often limited by their selectivity to target analytes, molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are intriguing methods to overcome such disadvantages. MIPs are three-dimensional polymer networks that can identify a target molecule selectively (Wang et al., 2020). This is done by constructing template molecules with functional monomers and cross-linking agents in a covalent or non-covalent manner (Mohiuddin et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021). The recognition cavities created after the removal of the template are complementary to the target analyte. The recognition cavities contain special functional groups that interact specifically with the target molecules. The synthesis methods for MIPs are diverse to include bulk polymerization, precipitation polymerization, suspension polymerization, and the sol–gel method. The molecular imprinting sol–gel method is a widely used option due to the fast mass transfer rate and high affinity. However, the traditional silica-based imprinted material shows internal extension resistance. This leads to a low adsorption capacity and slow recognition dynamics accompanied by the decrease in the binding rate. Mesoporous silica materials have received great attention in the field of nanotechnology for their large surface area, ordered structure, and uniform tunable pore size. (Amiri and Milani-Hosseini, 2019; Amiri and Hosseini, 2020). As the carrier of molecular imprinting sites, mesoporous silica can accelerate the speed of adsorption and separation to bind the imprinted molecules with the mesoporous material pore wall for rapid recognition of analytes (Zhang and Chen, 2016; Zhang et al., 2015). Accordingly, it is anticipated that the use of such mesoporous structured MIPs composite systems will help address the issue of traditional silica imprinting. The mesoporous structure in the imprinting layer can be generated by cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB). CTAB can be used as a structure-directing agent to assemble a meso-structured compound under basic conditions during the condensation of the silica precursor (Susanti and Hasanah, 2021). Mesoporous structures are eventually obtained by removing the CTAB through calcination or extraction (Susanti and Hasanah, 2021).

In this work, mesoporous silica-based MIPs coated on CDs (CDs@m-MIP) were manufactured and employed for the first time as the fluorescence probes for the determination of TRI in water samples in a highly selective and sensitive manner. The newly developed fluorescent probe (CDs@m-MIP) rebinds TRI more selectively than the other structural counterparts (e.g., bisphenol A, 17β-estradiol, 2,4 dinitrophenol, naphthalene, and triclocarban). This sensor (CDs@m-MIP) is both less expensive and more environmentally sustainable than chromatographic approaches (Jia et al., 2017). The chromatographic techniques are generally based on multistage sample preparation, expensive instrumentation, and time consuming separation procedures (Zhang and Chen, 2016). On the other hand, the proposed sensor (CDs@m-MIP) does not require any complex pre-separation procedure with many attractive properties such as being simple, quick, and reliable. Moreover, the CDs have been synthesized from orange juice in a more environment-friendly synthetic route to avoid the large consumption of toxic and expensive chemicals. Because of the electron-transfer induced fluorescence quenching process, this sensing approach may provide an effective and efficient option for rapid monitoring of some specific target analytes such as TRI.

A list of reagent chemicals including TRI, triclocarban (TCC), bisphenol A (BPA), 17β-estradiol (E2), 2,4 dinitrophenol (DNP), naphthalene (NAP), anthracene (ANT), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), and tetra ethoxysilane (TEOS) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) and ammonium hydroxide was obtained from Loba Chemie (Mumbai, India). A 1.0 mM standard stock solution of TRI was prepared in methanol and stored at 4 °C. The working standard solutions of TRI were prepared daily in the 1.72–138 nM range by further dilution of the standard stock solution with triply distilled water (TDW: SG Works, India), followed by sonication for degassing prior to use.

The fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu RF-5301 PC spectrofluorophotometer equipped with a quartz cell. The slit widths of both excitation and emission monochromators were set to 10 nm with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The fluorescence quantum yield was quantified relative to the standard solution of 9,10-diphenylanthracene in ethanol (QY value = 0.95) as a standard (Morris et al., 1976). 9,10-diphenylanthracene was chosen as a standard for fluorescence quantum yield because its range of excitation (275–380 nm) and emission wavelength (390–500 nm) corroborated with that of CDs and CDs@m-MIP-TRI (excitation wavelength-350 nm and emission wavelength-450 nm). Quantum yield values of CDs and CDs@m-MIP were measured as 0.62 and 0.57, respectively by using standard 9,10-diphenylanthracene. UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained by a UV–Vis 1600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were acquired on a RXIFT-IR (Perkin Elmer, Japan). The sample analysis was conducted by a HPLC (Waters, USA: Model 1525 Binary solvent pump, 2998 photodiode array detector, Breeze 2 software, and Spherisorb 5 μm ODS2 packing analytical column (4.6 × 250 mm)). The PDA wavelength was set in the range of 190–800 nm, while the analogue output channel was set at the wavelength of 275 nm. An isocratic elution was performed with solvent A (MeOH) and B (H2O) (9:1; v/v) with a flow rate of 1 mL/min. All the prepared solutions were filtered through Ultipor N Nylon 6,6 membranes (0.2 μm/0.47 mm). The mobile phase was degassed before injection onto HPLC-PDA. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) imaging and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) were performed on a Model JEM 2100 plus (JEOL, Japan). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was done using a Hitachi STA7300 thermogravimetric analyzer to assess thermal degradation curves of the MIP. The weight loss of synthesized materials (CDs vs. CDs@m-MIP) was recorded in a temperature range from 35 to 800 °C at a ramping rate of 10 °C/min with the continuous supply of nitrogen. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and structure parameters of CDs@m-MIP were determined using a Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area analyzer (Microtrac Belsorp Mini-II, Bel, Japan, Inc). The pH was measured using a Delux pH meter 101 (Electronics India). A REMI RM-12C centrifuge (REMI Laboratory Instruments, India) was used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of mesoporous silica imprinted and non-imprinted carbon dots

ce:section.ce:para: The hydrothermal carbonization of the major constituents of orange juice (e.g., glucose, sucrose, citric acid, fructose, and ascorbic acid) has been used to generate CDs. Oranges were purchased from a local market (Patiala, Punjab, India). Before use, they were processed into juice using an electric juicer. Initially, 50 mL of orange juice was added to a 100 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave. The autoclave was maintained at 200 °C for 6 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the brown suspension was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to remove large particles followed by the filtration through 0.22 μm syringe filter. The synthesized CDs were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C until use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of meso-porous silica imprinted carbon dots (CDs@m-MIP) and non-imprinted carbon dots (CDs@m-NIP)
ce:section.ce:para: First, 1 mL of CD solution was added to 10 mL of TDW, and the mixture was sonicated for 10 min. Then, 200 μL of APTES and 40 mg of triclosan (TRI) were added and stirred for 30 min in the dark. Then, 2 mL of CTAB (0.2 M) was added in the above reaction mixture. After stirring for 30 min, 800 μL of TEOS and 1.5 mL of ammonia solution were added in the mixture and were stirred continuously for 12 h in the dark. The obtained product was then centrifuged for about 15 min (10,000 rpm). The products were then washed with a mixture of methanol/CH3COOH (9:1, v/v) to remove TRI and CTAB and then dried in oven to obtain CDs@m-MIP. Likewise, mesoporous non-imprinted polymers (CDs@m-NIP) were obtained in the same manner, but without the addition of TRI.

To a 5 mL volumetric flask, 2 mg of CDs@m-MIP, 500 μL of phosphate buffer solution (pH = 7), and 1 mL of TRI sample (or standard solution) were added. A volume of 5 mL was obtained by adding TDW. After incubation for 6 min, the fluorescence intensity was measured at 450 nm with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm.

Different water samples were collected including sewage wastewater, river water, and tap water. The effluent from sewage wastewater sample was collected from the Punjabi University, Patiala (Punjab, India) in December 2020. The river water was collected from the Tawi river (Jammu and Kashmir, India) during the months of November and December (2020), and tap water was taken from our laboratory. All the collected water samples were filtered through Whatman filter paper (pore size 11 μm) and nylon-6,6 membrane filters (pore size 0.2 μm) before analysis, and they were stored at 4 °C for further use.

To obtain structural information about the CDs, the FTIR spectra were obtained (Fig. 1 ). A broad peak is seen at 3346 cm−1, which is ascribable to OH stretching vibrations. The absorption bands at 2331 cm−1 and 1630 cm−1 correspond to CH and CO group stretching vibrations, respectively (Fig. 1a).

For CDs@m-MIP (with TRI), the peaks around 1052 and 794 cm−1 are ascribable to asymmetric stretching of Si–O–Si and the bending vibration of the SiO groups, respectively (Fig. 1b), indicating the presence of TEOS. The bands around 3229 cm−1 and 1510 cm−1 also confirmed the presence of APTES as they can be assigned to the stretching and bending vibrations of NH, respectively. The characteristic peak at around 1445–1408 cm−1 can reflect the aromatic stretching vibration of TRI (Fig. 1b). After the removal of TRI by solvent elution, the characteristic peak in the aromatic region (1445–1407 cm−1) of TRI disappeared in the FTIR spectrum of CDs@m-MIP without TRI (Fig. 1c). Therefore, this observation suggests that TRI molecules have been successfully imprinted into the matrix. Further, they have been effectively washed out during the extraction process. The absorption peaks for CDs at 3346.34 and 2331.11 cm−1 become weakened significantly in CDs@m-MIP (Fig. 1b and c). This suggests that the imprinted layer should be successfully fabricated on the surface of the CDs.

The morphologies of the CDs, CDs@m-MIP, and CDs@m-NIP were investigated by HR-TEM (Fig. 2 ). The spherical CD particles (average diameter of 2–5 ± 0.94 nm) were well dispersed. Their size distribution was found between 2.8 and 7 nm when confirmed further by DLS (Fig. S1). After the polymer layer was anchored on the surface of CDs, the sizes of CDs@m-MIP and NIP were observed to be 60–80 nm. The shell thickness of the MIP layer was about 30–40 nm. Both the CDs@m-MIP and CDs@m-NIP had a spherical shape with the CDs trapped in the center of the particle. The presence of worm-like channels indicates the existence of a mesoporous structure in the CDs@m-MIP and CDs@m-NIP (Fig. 2c-f). As such, CDs@m-MIP was effectively synthesized with CD compartments as a sensitive fluorescent unit. Further, the imprinted mesoporous silica shell fabricated on CDs serves as a selective recognition unit for TRI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherm analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The mesoporous silica structure of CDs@m-MIP was confirmed by nitrogen adsorption experiments. As shown in Fig. S2a, CDs@m-MIP is mainly mesoporous in nature with a physisorption isotherm of Type IV with H3 loop features. The BET surface area was 280.2 ± 0.1 m2 g−1. Details of the BET calculation and interpretation of the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda plot are described in section S.1 of the Supplementary Information. Although a mesoporous structure with a high intensity at an average diameter of 2.2 nm was obtained, a significant number of pores are in the range of 4 to 8 nm from the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) pore size distribution curve (Fig. S2b). It is noted that the molar volume (V) for TRI is 194 cm3 mol−1, and that the polar surface is 29 Å2. Because chlorine atoms are on the aromatic rings pointing outside, the polar area is taken to be equal to the actual surface area. Hence, the corresponding hydraulic diameter, defined as 4*V/A, is equal to 4.4 nm. This type of structure is expected to favorably lower the mass transfer resistance while increasing the corresponding site accessibility. In addition, these results suggest that the material shape is now better suited for the rapid transfer rate and higher binding capacity of TRI.

EDX was employed to assess the completeness of synthesis for the prepared materials (CDs and CDs@m-MIP) (Fig. S3 and Table S1). The appearance of C and O signals in the EDX spectrum indicates the formation of CDs. Compared to CDs, the EDX spectrum of CDs@m-MIP shows three elements: carbon, oxygen, and silicon. These results confirmed the fabrication of silica imprinting layer obtained from the sol gel condensation of APTES and TEOS on the surface of CDs.

The thermal stability of the synthesized materials was assessed by TGA (Fig. 3 ). For CDs, the initial mass loss of 50 % was observed between 35 and 115 °C to reflect the loss of water molecules and other species attached through weak hydrogen interactions present within the nanodots. A further mass loss of 18.5 % between 500 and 590 °C took place due to the decomposition of organic functional groups in CDs (Mehta et al., 2014). For CDs@m-MIP, a mass loss of 15 % was observed in a temperature range from 35 °C to 100 °C in association with the loss of residual or adsorbed water molecules. In the range of 240–400 °C, mass loss of about 12 % may have taken place due to the thermal decomposition of the polymer layer. These results indicate that an imprinted polymer layer should be successfully fabricated on CDs to manifest the good thermal stability of the CDs@m-MIP.

The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs, CDs@m-MIP, and CDs@m-NIP were analyzed. As shown in Fig. 4a, the fluorescence emission of CDs was strongly dependent on excitation wavelength. As the excitation wavelength increased, the emission of the CDs showed a red-shift with a decrease in intensity. This arises both from the optical selection of differently sized nanoparticles (a quantum effect) and primarily from the different emissive traps present on the surface due to the presence of different functional groups (Xu et al., 2016). At certain excitation wavelengths, some emissive sites would be excited and fluoresce with the excitation dependent behavior of the emission spectra (Bao et al., 2011). As a maximum fluorescence intensity of CDs was observed at a 350 nm excitation wavelength, this was chosen as an excitation wavelength for further experiments. The CDs@m-NIP showed strong fluorescence emission, while CDs@MIP with TRI shows a weak fluorescent signal. However, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@m-MIP was restored after the extraction of the template, nearly meeting the level observed for CDs@m-NIP (Fig. 4b).

To obtain the excellent sensing performance of CDs@m-MIP against TRI, the effects of process variables (e.g., response time, mass concentration, and pH effect) on its performance has been explored as below.

The effect of the response time was first investigated to evaluate the minimum time of template molecules to access the imprinted sites. In Fig. S4, the fluorescence intensity reached a minimum after 6 min and then remained constantly. To learn more about the effect of response time, the experimental data were evaluated using polygonal line model (Heynderickx et al., 2020). The details of the polygonal line model can be found in section S.2 of the Supplementary Information. The presence of the mesoporous structure was helpful to shorten the response times for CDs@m-MIP as it lowers the mass transfer resistance. The generation of imprinted sites in the pores of the mesoporous silica, as supported by the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm analysis, offered high accessibility for TRI molecules. Accordingly, the optimum response time of 6 min was chosen for further experiments.

The mass concentration of CDs@m-MIP has an apparent effect on the fluorescence quenching efficiency (Xu et al., 2013). The effect of CDs@m-MIP quantities was hence examined in the presence of TRI using 5 mL solution in the concentration range of 100–500 mg/L. Afterwards, the fluorescence response was recorded after 6 min at room temperature. As the maximum quenching was observed at 400 mg/L, it was chosen as an optimum quantity of CDs@m-MIP (Fig. S5). The experimental data was further evaluated using polygonal line model to examine the effect of mass concentration of CDs@m-MIP on the fluorescence quenching (Section S.2 of Supplementary Information).

The pH of the solution can play an important role in regulating the fluorescence intensity of CDs@m-MIP through interaction between the three-dimensional structure and the charge of TRI. The influence of pH on the CDs@m-MIP was investigated in the pH range 3–11 using a 5 mL solution containing 2 mg CDs@m-MIP against 51.8 nM of TRI with the adjustment of pH (using 0.1 M solutions of HCl or NaOH) (Jalili and Amjadi, 2018). After 6 min, the response of the fluorescence was measured at room temperature. As shown in Fig. S6, the pH effect was most prominent at pH 7. At lower pH, hydrogen bonding between the CDs@m-MIP and TRI was weakened due to the protonation of CDs@m-MIP. On the other hand, at higher pH, the sensitivity also decreased due to the ionization of the TRI molecule (pKa of TRI is 7.9–8.1) (Revitt et al., 2015). In addition, at higher pH, the ionization of mesoporous silica layer can damage the structure of binding sites (Chullasat et al., 2018). Thus, pH 7 was chosen as the optimal pH for further experiments.

The synthesized CDs@m-MIP was used to selectively recognize and detect the TRI as illustrated in Fig. 5 . Photo-induced electron transfer from the excited state of the CDs to the TRI should take place upon rebinding of TRI as presented as the fluorescence quenching mechanism. The fluorescence in the CDs is suggested to arise from the radiative recombination of electron-hole pairs (Jalili and Amjadi, 2015). In the absence of TRI, the CDs@m-MIP showed a strong blue emission at 450 nm on excitation at 350 nm. Upon the addition of TRI, the amino group of APTES present in the binding sites of CDs@m-MIP can interact with the hydroxyl groups of TRI to form a Meisenheimer complex via hydrogen bonding (Jia et al., 2017). This interaction can be confirmed by UV–Vis spectra (Fig. S7). In addition to spectra of APTES and TRI (Fig. S7b and c), a new absorbance peak appeared at 390 nm (Fig. S7a) after the addition of APTES into the TRI solution. This interaction leads to electron transfer between CDs and TRI to significantly suppress the radiative recombination of the electron hole pair (Jalili and Amjadi, 2015). As shown in Fig. S7b, the UV absorption band of TRI is close to the band gap of the CDs. The electrons present in the conduction band of CDs can jump into the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of TRI molecules (Fig. S8). This transfer of electrons to the LUMO of TRI can then lead to the fluorescence quenching of CDs. Conversely, energy transfer is not possible in fluorescence quenching as there is no spectral overlap between the absorption spectrum of TRI and emission spectrum of CDs@m-MIP (S7c). Fig. S9 shows the lifetime decay curves for the fluorescence emission of CDs@m-MIP and CDs@m-MIP with TRI fitted into a three component exponential–decay curve. The average lifetime of CDs@m-MIP is about 4.77 ns while its fluorescence lifetime is 4.26 ns after the addition of TRI. This little change signifies that the quenching may proceed in a static manner.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of the CDs@m-MIP and CDs@m-NIP sensors towards TRI
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence response of the CDs@m-MIP was investigated based on the change in the fluorescence intensity across varying concentrations of TRI (e.g., from 0 to 138 nM). Accordingly, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs@m-MIP was affected significantly. The sensing performance of the CDs@m-NIP was also investigated by the same procedure as discussed above for CDs@m-MIP (Fig. 6b). The change in fluorescence intensity of CDs@m-NIP was significantly smaller by about one order of magnitude than the CDs@m-MIP. The higher quenching efficiency of CDs@m-MIP likely reflects the presence of specific imprinted cavities for TRI formed during the polymerization process.

To describe the quenching mechanism, the fluorescence quenching data were analyzed according to the Stern Volmer relation (1): here, F0, F, KSV and [TRI] are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of a given concentration of TRI, the Stern-Volmer constant, and the concentration of quencher, respectively:
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K SV
⋅
TRI

The Stern-Volmer constant, KSV, indicates the sensitivity of the fluorophore to a quencher. Fig. 7 displays the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of CDs@m-MIP (or CDs@m-NIP) and TRI concentrations in the range of 1.72 to 138 nM. The Stern-Volmer quenching constants for CDs@m-MIP and NIP were (1.10 ± 0.01) × 10−2 and (1.47 ± 0.03) × 10−3, respectively. The R2 values were 0.995 and 0.987, respectively. The details of the Stern-Volmer modeling can be found in section S.3 of the Supplementary Information. The imprinting factor (IF), which is defined as the ratio of KSV of CDs@m-MIP and CDs@m-NIP (IF = KSV (CDs@m-MIP)/KSV (CDs@m-NIP)) was used to evaluate the selectivity of the CDs@m-MIP sensor. The computed IF value of 7.48 ± 0.17 indicates that the specific cavities of CDs@m-MIP are capable of obtaining almost an order or magnitude higher selectivity for the determination of TRI.

The analytical performance of the developed sensor was evaluated in terms of linearity, limit of detection limit (LOD), and intra-day precision. The CDs@m-MIP exhibited linear fluorescence quenching for TRI in the concentration range of 1.72–138 nM with a coefficient of determination of 0.991. The LOD of 1.08 nM was estimated based on the ratio of
3 δ
b
, where δ is the standard deviation of the blank signal and b as the slope of calibration curve (Zhang and Chen, 2016). Under the optimized condition, a good reproducibility is confirmed with the computed relative standard deviation (RSD) value of 0.70–1.24 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the CDs@m-MIP and CDs@m-NIP
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the CDs@m-MIP sensor was also investigated by comparing the fluorescence intensity of CDs@m-MIP after interaction with the target analyte (TRI) and with other potential interfering substances. Potential interfering substances are reported to be triclocarban (TCC), bisphenol A, (BPA) 17β-estradiol (E2), 2,4 dinitrophenol (DNP), naphthalene (NAP), and anthracene (ANT) (Peckova-Schwarzova et al., 2015; Rodriguez-Mozaz et al., 2004). The chemical structures of these analogs are given in Fig. S10. The fluorescence intensities of the CDs@m-MIP before and after the addition of these compounds were recorded. In Fig. S11, the change in fluorescence quenching, when assessed upon TRI rebinding, was considerably higher for CDs@m-MIP than for the other substances. This can be explained by the formation of target analyte-specific recognition sites particular to the template created during the synthesis process. The TRI-specific recognition cavities were generated on the surface of CDs@m-MIP so that the TRI could undergo significant change in the fluorescence intensity by strong binding to such sites. The competing molecules (including TCC, BPA, E2, DNP, NAP, and ANT) cause low fluorescence quenching when compared to TRI. It should be noted that BPA, E2, and DNP could interact with functional monomer (APTES) by H-bonding. However, as the recognition sites of the imprinted cavities are not accessible to them, much lower quenching of fluorescence intensity was observed. In the case of NAP and ANT, very little or no fluorescence quenching was found because they have no functional groups that interact with APTES.

The fluorescence intensity of CDs@m-NIP was also investigated in the presence of these substances. No obvious change in the fluorescence intensity was found after the addition of these compounds to the CDs@m-NIP. This suggests that CDs@m-NIP could not effectively bind TRI and other compounds to detect TRI with good selectivity and sensitivity (Fig. S11).

Based on the experimental data from Fig. S11, the point values for the Stern-Volmer constant (KSV) can be calculated using Eq. (1). The plot of the obtained KSV values on CDs@m-MIP (and CDs@m-NIP) indicates that some molecules (NAP and ANT) do not show any interaction with the CDs@m-MIP nor CDs@m-NIP (close to origin) (Fig. 8 ). As this is explained by the absence of hydroxyl groups, a Meisenheimer complex cannot be formed with the sensor (it concerns polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons with fused rings). Hydroxyl group bearing molecules (BPA, E2, and DNP) show no significant difference in sensitivity on CDs@m-NIP. Therefore, the CDs@m-NIP is non-selective towards BPA, E2, and DNP. The point for TCC is approximately on the same value for compounds BPA, E2, and DNP on CDs@m-NIP, while it shows a higher value on CDs@m-MIP. A possible reason for the latter results can be the high similarity between TCC and TRI when it comes to the present chlorides, connected to the aromatic rings (Fig. S10). It is visually clear that CDs@m-MIP displays the highest KSV value for TRI to reflect the highest quenching (sensing) effect against TRI. It also illustrates the selective sensing capacity of CDs@m-MIP towards TRI. The Ksv plot for fluorescence data is presented with the data points in Fig. 8 for TRI, TCC, BPA, E2, DNP, ANT, and NAP. These data points for the KSV values of E2, BPA, DNP, TCC, and TRI (in increasing order) are readily visible with the enhanced selectivity of analytes towards CDs@m-MIP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and analytical performance of the CDs@m-MIP sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the synthesized CDs@m-MIP sensor was investigated by the repetitive measurements of fluorescence emission intensity at room temperature. Fig. S12 shows there is no significant change in the fluorescence intensity of the CDs@m-MIP sensor over 120 min. This indicates that the CDs@m-MIP was uniformly dispersed in the solution without obvious settling or agglomeration. The stability of the sensor was further investigated by storing the CDs@m-MIP in a refrigerator at 4 °C up to 1 month. The fluorescence intensity did not show significant change even after 1 month, indicating the physical stability and chemical inertness of CDs@m-MIP due to the highly stable cross-linked polymer network.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility and regeneration of CDs@m-MIP sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The reproducibility of a synthesized CDs@m-MIP sensor was investigated by preparing five different batches of CDs@m-MIP under identical conditions as described in Section 2.3. The as-prepared CDs@m-MIP was used to detect TRI at a concentration of 51.8 nM under optimum conditions (pH = 7; incubation time = 6 min; dosage amount = 2 mg). As shown in Fig. S13a, the developed CDs@m-MIP showed good reproducibility for the determination of TRI with RSDs between 0.52 and 2.62 %.

Reusability is an important criterion for qualifying a fluorescent sensor since it is an essential criterion for economic feasibility. To assess the reusability of the sensor, adsorption-desorption cycles were tested over a total of 15 cycles by monitoring the changes in the fluorescence intensity of CDs@m-MIP. CDs@m-MIP (2 mg) was added into a 5 mL solution containing TRI to measure the fluorescence intensity after 6 min. The CDs@m-MIP particles were washed with MeOH/acetic acid (9:1 v/v) to remove the TRI after recovery by centrifuge. As the number of reuses increased, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@m-MIP sensor slightly decreased. The CDs@m-MIP sensor maintained good reusability, retaining over 80 % of its original fluorescence intensity up to the tenth cycle. The recovery values obtained over 10 extraction cycles of TRI ranged from 98.69 % (1st), 94.38 % (5th), and 83.18 % (10th).A further decrease in its intensity was observed from 79.35 % (11th) to 70.41 % (15th cycle) (Fig. S13b). This decrease in fluorescence intensity of CDs@m-MIP may reflect the saturation of surface-active sites or the loss of sensing probe during washing. Furthermore, it is likely that not all the TRI is removed during each cycle. More effort or a better TRI desorption technique is therefore necessary to make these sensors reusable for >10 cycles. Overall, the results indicate that CDs@m-MIP has good reusability with strong chemical and mechanical stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application of CDs@m-MIP for TRI detection
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs@m-MIP material was applied for the detection of TRI in different real water samples including sewage water, river water, and tap water. The TRI was detected in the sewage water sample at a concentration of 46.2 nM by the CDs@m-MIP fluorescence method. However, TRI was not detected or was below the method detection limit in river and tap water samples. Hence, the suitability of the method in these matrices was determined by measuring their analytical recovery after adding different amounts of TRI based on the standard addition method (by spiking known quantities of 3.45, 17.2, and 34.5 nM solutions). As shown in Table 1 , the recovery values of TRI from tap water and river water were 98.6–99.5 % (RSD% = 0.70–1.18 %) and 94.7–96.2 (RSD% = 1.05–1.24 %), respectively. These values indicate that the proposed sensor is satisfactory and can be employed for the detection of TRI in real water samples.

The practicality of the developed fluorescence method was cross-validated against the HPLC. The HPLC chromatograms obtained for the standard and sewage water sample spiked with triclosan (20 μM) are shown in Fig. S14. The results indicated that HPLC technique is consistent with the developed method for the detection of TRI in water samples.

The performance of the proposed method was examined by comparing the results with those obtained from other reported methods (fluorescence, electrochemical and high-performance liquid chromatography). As shown in Table S2, the CDs@m-MIP showed a detection limit (LOD) of 1.08 nM for TRI. Silicon doped graphene quantum dots combined with Ru (III) showed a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.54 nM, which is less than the reported fluorescent probe (Du et al., 2019). Similarly, the LODs obtained by the electrochemical (Feng et al., 2018) and HPLC method (Alshishani et al., 2019) were also less than the developed CDs@m-MIP method. However, the reusability of the materials described in these methods is not reported. In view of cost benefits and environmental protection, the reusability of the material must be validated. The developed CDs@m-MIP can be reused up to 10-times without significant change in its fluorescence intensity. Moreover, the developed method showed fast response (6 min) and a good recovery in the range of 95.74–99.29 % (RSD <3 %). The introduction of molecular imprinting technology endowed CDs@m-MIP sensor with high thermal and chemical stability and superior selectivity so that the built sensor system can avoid the complicated pre-processing with the short analysis time. It is nonetheless still desirable to put more efforts to improve the sensitivity of the CDs@m-MIP while retaining the high selectivity.

CDs@m-MIP was synthesized for the sensitive and selective fluorescence determination of TRI in water samples. The developed sensor demonstrated high selectivity for TRI other than the competitive analogues based on photo-induced electron transfer fluorescence quenching. The CDs@m-MIP showed linear fluorescence quenching for TRI in the concentration range of 1.72–138 nM. The CDs@m-MIP showed fast response (6 min), a detection limit of 1.08 nM for TRI, and can be reused up to 10 times with relatively little drop off in performance. The developed CDs@m-MIP showed excellent recovery of TRI (94.7–99.5 %). Moreover, the method was able to demonstrate the detection of TRI in real sewage water samples. The simple, fast, and reliable CDs@m-MIP sensor developed in this work is an efficient option for the selective and sensitive sensing of many different analytes (including TRI) in real environmental water samples.

Shikha Bhogal: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Irshad Mohiuddin: Investigation. Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing.Ashok Kumar Malik: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Richard J.C. Brown: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Philippe M. Heynderickx: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Ki-Hyun Kim: Conceptualization, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Kuldeep Kaur: Conceptualization, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors (SB, IM, and AKM) acknowledge the UGC-SAP and Chemistry Department, Punjabi University, Patiala, for providing lab and instrument facilities. AKM thanks the UGC, New Delhi for a mid-career award grant. KK thanks Mata Gujri College, Fatehgarh Sahib, Punjab for providing lab facilities. KHK acknowledges support provided by a grant from the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science and ITC (MSIT) of the Korean government (Grant No: 2021R1A3B1068304). PMH would like to thank the Research and Development Program of Ghent University Global Campus (GUGC), Korea.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dummy molecularly imprinted polymers-capped CdTe quantum dots for the fluorescent sensing of 2, 4, 6-trinitrotoluene
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Xu, S., H. Lu, J. Li, X. Song, A. Wang, L. Chen and S. Han (2013). "Dummy molecularly imprinted polymers-capped CdTe quantum dots for the fluorescent sensing of 2, 4, 6-trinitrotoluene." ACS applied materials & interfaces 5(16): 8146-8154.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Xu et al., 2016

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-pot synthesis of carbon dots-embedded molecularly imprinted polymer for specific recognition of sterigmatocystin in grains
Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Xu, L., G. Fang, M. Pan, X. Wang and S. Wang (2016). "One-pot synthesis of carbon dots-embedded molecularly imprinted polymer for specific recognition of sterigmatocystin in grains." Biosensors and Bioelectronics 77: 950-956.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhang and Chen, 2016

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence probe based on hybrid mesoporous silica/quantum dot/molecularly imprinted polymer for detection of tetracycline
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang, L. and L. Chen (2016). "Fluorescence probe based on hybrid mesoporous silica/quantum dot/molecularly imprinted polymer for detection of tetracycline." ACS applied materials & interfaces 8(25): 16248-16256.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhang et al., 2014

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous determination of pharmaceutical and personal care products in wastewater by capillary electrophoresis with head-column field-amplified sample stacking
Anal. Methods

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang, Z., D. Zhang and X. Zhang (2014). "Simultaneous determination of pharmaceutical and personal care products in wastewater by capillary electrophoresis with head-column field-amplified sample stacking." Analytical Methods 6(19): 7978-7983.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhang et al., 2015

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum dots based mesoporous structured imprinting microspheres for the sensitive fluorescent detection of phycocyanin
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang, Z., J. Li, X. Wang, D. Shen and L. Chen (2015). "Quantum dots based mesoporous structured imprinting microspheres for the sensitive fluorescent detection of phycocyanin." ACS applied materials & interfaces 7(17): 9118-9127.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhang et al., 2021

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A semi-covalent molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensor for highly specific recognition and optosensing of bisphenol A
Analyt. Meth.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang J, Wang H, Xu L, Xu Z. A semi-covalent molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensor for highly specific recognition and optosensing of bisphenol A. Analytical Methods. 2021;13(1):133-40.
